result:
There are mornings when the city feels like a library, spines upright, stories sealed, waiting for a practiced hand to pull one life from the shelf. On those mornings I walk toward the river, listening for the whisper of gears beneath the pavement, the nearly forgotten chorus of the city’s secret machines. They hum in sympathy with heartbeats, they synchronize with pigeons, they remember the names of sailors and gardeners and bakers whose footprints once softened this concrete. History is not a distant ledger but a change jar rattling beside your keys, coins warmed by today’s impatient hands seeking rest.

Because I collect stray questions, my pockets shimmer whenever the wind changes. Yesterday a postcard arrived from a friend studying moss in Iceland. She wondered whether ecosystems dream differently after humans leave the room. That night I imagined lichen murmuring syllables, trading spores for lullabies, waiting for glaciers to hum again. I pictured the planet exhaling without embarrassment, shoulders unclenched, tectonic plates stretching like students after long lectures. In my notebook I drew a theater whose curtains were auroras, whose audience consisted of seats collecting the warmth of absent bodies. Maybe ecosystems dream by memorizing departures until returns arrive again.

Every afternoon at three, neighborhood children chalk constellations onto the asphalt. They skip telescopes, inviting passing clouds to revise their lines. I watch from the balcony and remember the summer I mapped the subway inside a spiral notebook. Each stop received a myth, every tunnel a
